citation: Marín, K., & Castellano, J. (2023). High-speed running distance and frequency in football training: When and how are they stimulated in a microcycle? International Journal of Sports Science & Coaching, 18(4), 1132–1141.
abstract: This study described the distribution of distance and frequency of high-speed running (HSR >21 km/h), very high-speed running (VHSR >24 km/h), and sprint (SPR >27 km/h) in professional football training across microcycles (Mi) of 4, 5, 6, and 7 days between matches. GPS data were collected from 23 La Liga players across 28 microcycles during the 2018–19 season. Central training days (MD-3, MD-4) accumulated the greatest high-speed locomotive demands regardless of microcycle duration, while MD-2 consistently showed the least stimulation. Longer microcycles (Mi7) had significantly higher HSR values on MD-4 and MD-5 compared to shorter microcycles.
tldr: Professional football teams follow an inverted-U weekly load pattern: high-speed running is concentrated in the mid-week acquisition sessions (MD-3 and MD-4), tapers before match day (with MD-2 as the lowest stimulus day), and is kept low immediately post-match. Total accumulated HSR volume does not increase proportionally when more days are available between matches, but longer microcycles push high-speed work further from the upcoming match (e.g., MD-5 in 7-day weeks). Staff should program sprint and high-speed running deliberately in mid-week to optimize adaptation while preserving freshness for competition.
methods: Descriptive, observational design. Twenty-three male professional players from one La Liga club were monitored via 10 Hz Catapult GPS units across 28 competitive microcycles (2018–19 season). Microcycles were classified by days between matches (Mi4, Mi5, Mi6, Mi7). HSR (>21 km/h), VHSR (>24 km/h), and SPR (>27 km/h) were quantified by total distance (m) and frequency (occurrences) per session. One-way ANOVA with Bonferroni/Dunnett's T3 post hoc tests; effect sizes reported as Cohen's d. Mixed linear models used for MD-day comparisons.
findings: No significant differences in total accumulated HSR, VHSR, or SPR volume across microcycle durations (Mi4–Mi7). Regardless of microcycle length, central days (MD-4, MD-3) received significantly greater locomotive demands than post-match (MD-7, MD-6) and pre-match (MD-2, MD-1) sessions, with MD-2 requiring the least stimulation (p<0.05). In 7-day microcycles, MD-4 and MD-5 showed significantly higher HSR/VHSR/SPR than shorter microcycles. MD-3 was consistently the day with the greatest stimulation across all microcycle lengths. Substantial intra-session sprint variability was noted, attributable to position, fitness status, and competition participation.
limitations: Single team and single coaching philosophy limits generalizability. No internal load metrics (e.g., heart rate, RPE) were included to contextualize physiological response. Testing/readiness and match performance data were not integrated. Large SD in sprint outputs reflects high inter-individual variability.
practicalImplications: High-speed running (HSR, VHSR, SPR) should be programmed primarily on mid-week days (MD-3 and MD-4), distanced from both the preceding and upcoming match to allow recovery and avoid compromising match-day readiness. When more days are available between matches, push high-speed work earlier (MD-5) rather than simply adding more total volume. MD-2 should consistently be the lowest-stimulus session. Coaches should adjust the timing of sprint stimuli based on microcycle length, not just overall volume.
athleteDev: For developing football athletes, this study supports the principle of horizontal alternation within the weekly microcycle: recovery sessions post-match, acquisition sessions mid-week (with sprint and high-speed running emphasis), and tapering pre-match. Building tolerance to high-speed running across the week at appropriate distances and frequencies may serve a protective function against injury and support long-term speed development.
rtp: The study underscores that MD-2 is the most protected session in professional programming. For athletes returning from injury, this session structure provides a logical integration framework: return players to mid-week sessions with graduated HSR exposure (beginning at MD-3 or MD-4 level intensities), progressing as they demonstrate tolerance, before full participation in match-day activities.

citation: Lacki, J. F. (2021). High training/competition ratio, less incidence of injury? Professional football calendar exploration. Apunts Sports Medicine, 56(209), 100338.
abstract: This 12-season prospective analysis of an Argentine professional football club (Defensa y Justicia, 2003–2014) investigated whether a high training-to-competition ratio is associated with lower injury incidence. The team competed in a single tournament (38 rounds/season) at approximately 3.85 matches/month, yielding a training/competition ratio of 8.4 training hours per match hour. Over 12 seasons, 797 injuries were recorded across 4,710 training hours and 715 competition hours. Overall injury incidence was 4.2 per 1,000 hours, compared to 30.5 (competition) and 5.8 (training) per 1,000 hours in UEFA Champions League data. Sixty-five percent of injuries resolved within 7 days.
tldr: Football teams competing in fewer matches per season with longer recovery-training cycles between games (5–8 days) show substantially lower injury incidence rates than European clubs playing in multiple concurrent competitions. This South American club's injury rate (4.2/1,000 h total; 2.3/1,000 h training; 20.5/1,000 h competition) was well below UEFA benchmarks (5.8 and 30.5/1,000 h respectively), and 65% of injuries resolved within one week—compared to only 27% in UEFA data. More training relative to competition may improve fitness, build structural resilience, and reduce severe injury burden.
methods: Prospective longitudinal injury surveillance over 12 seasons (2003–2014) for one Argentine First Division club. Daily medical records documented all injuries (defined per Dvorak & Junge 2000 as missed training/match time with anatomical diagnosis). Injury severity classified using the Paus modification (Grade I: 1–7 days; II: 1–3 weeks; III: 3–8 weeks; IV: >8 weeks). Incidence calculated as injuries per 1,000 participation hours for training and competition separately. Data compared to UEFA Champions League and English FA Championship injury reports.
findings: Injury incidence rate: 4.2/1,000 h (combined), 2.3/1,000 h training, 20.5/1,000 h competition. Training/competition ratio: 8.4:1 (vs. 6.3:1 in UEFA 2016/17). Average absence per injury: 21.4 h. Injury burden: 90 days/1,000 h (vs. 130 days/1,000 h in UEFA Champions League). Grade I injuries (1–7 days): 65% of all injuries. Muscle injuries most common (44.5%), followed by knee (17.3%) and ankle (16%). Hamstrings comprised 53.5% of muscle injuries. Non-contact injuries: 57.1%. Surgical procedures required in only 1.24% of cases.
limitations: Single club and coaching staff across 12 seasons limits generalizability. Training load recorded by time only (no GPS), which reduces precision. Illness-related absences were not recorded as injuries, which may explain some of the lower incidence compared to European studies. The Argentine league structure (single tournament, two annual cycles) differs substantially from European multi-competition formats.
practicalImplications: Sports calendars allowing 5–8 days between competitions and a training/competition ratio of approximately 8:1 may function as a protective factor for professional footballers. Administrators and governing bodies planning fixture schedules should consider injury burden data when designing competition formats. Clubs competing in congested schedules should proactively plan recovery, manage training load, and monitor injury surveillance metrics. Muscle injuries—especially hamstrings—remain the primary target for prevention regardless of schedule.
athleteDev: Athletes in development programs who train more than they compete may benefit from greater structural adaptation and lower injury risk. The data support the idea that higher chronic training loads relative to competition can prepare athletes to tolerate the demands of matches. Periodization models that prioritize training volume and progressive loading during lower-competition periods may produce more robust and injury-resilient athletes.
rtp: The finding that 65% of injuries resolved within 7 days—compared to 27% in UEFA populations—suggests that lower accumulated fatigue from congested schedules may facilitate faster recovery and return to play. Adequate recovery-training cycles between competitions (5–8 days) may support more complete physiological restoration, reducing re-injury risk. Return-to-play protocols should account for the team's competition frequency as a contextual factor in managing return timelines.

citation: Iverson, G. L., Merz, Z. C., & Terry, D. P. (2022). High-school football and midlife brain health problems. Clinical Journal of Sport Medicine, 32(2), 86–94.
abstract: This cross-sectional online survey study examined whether middle-aged men (ages 35–55) who played high-school football experience worse brain health outcomes compared to men who played other contact sports, noncontact sports, or no sports. A total of 407 men were stratified by high-school sport history (football n=123, contact sports n=69, noncontact sports n=97, no sports n=118). Validated measures included the PHQ-8 for depression symptoms and BC-PSI for post-concussion-like symptoms. No significant group differences were found in lifetime mental health treatment, rates of depression, anxiety, concentration or memory problems, or post-concussion symptom totals. Football players reported significantly more lifetime concussions, higher rates of chronic pain and headache medication prescriptions, and more sleep problems in the past year.
tldr: Men who played high-school football (American) do not report meaningfully worse mental health or cognitive functioning in midlife compared to men who played other sports or no sports. While former football players had more lifetime concussions and higher rates of chronic pain and headache medication use, they did not differ from comparison groups on depression, anxiety, memory, or post-concussion symptoms. Sleep difficulties were more prevalent in the football group. These findings are consistent with six prior studies and do not support the idea that high-school football participation necessarily leads to poor brain health in middle age.
methods: Cross-sectional cohort study using an online survey via Amazon Mechanical Turk (mTurk). Participants: 407 US men aged 35–55. Groups: football (n=123), other contact sports (n=69), noncontact sports (n=97), no sports (n=118). Measures: PHQ-8 (depression), BC-PSI (post-concussion symptoms), and a custom survey on health history, current symptoms, concussion history, and medication use. Statistical analyses: one-way ANOVA, Kruskal-Wallis H tests, chi-square tests, Spearman/point-biserial correlations, and logistic regression. Validity items embedded to filter inattentive respondents.
findings: No significant group differences in: lifetime history of depression or anxiety medication, mental health treatment, rates of depression/anxiety/anger/concentration/memory/headache/chronic pain over the past year, PHQ-8 depression scores, or BC-PSI post-concussion symptom totals. Football players reported more lifetime concussions (mean 2.28 vs. 0.68–1.67 in other groups), higher rates of chronic pain medication prescription (66.7%), more headache medication prescriptions (26%), and greater sleep difficulties in the past year (39.0% vs. 20.6–30.5%). Predictors of memory problems: sleep difficulties (OR=3.19), anxiety (OR=2.90), concussion history (OR=2.33), and depression (OR=2.26); high-school football was not a significant predictor.
limitations: Self-reported data subject to recall and response bias. Consent language mentioning CTE may have introduced selection bias. Concussion history could not be independently verified. Multiple comparisons were not corrected for (though null findings dominate). The sample had a relatively high rate of depressive symptoms compared to the general population. Cross-sectional design prevents causal inference. All participants were current US residents which may limit demographic generalizability.
practicalImplications: High-school football participation alone does not appear to predict worse brain health in midlife. Clinicians working with former football players should be aware that sleep difficulties, chronic pain, and headaches may be more prevalent in this population and should be assessed and treated. Depression, anxiety, sleep problems, and concussion history are the primary predictors of memory complaints—not sport participation per se. Evidence-based treatments for these comorbid conditions can substantially improve function and quality of life.
athleteDev: These data provide reassurance that high-school-level football participation is not inherently linked to midlife cognitive or mental health deficits. For athlete development programs, this supports continued participation at the high-school level with appropriate concussion management protocols in place. The elevated concussion history in the football group underscores the ongoing importance of concussion education, recognition, and safe return-to-play protocols at the youth and high-school levels.
rtp: The study indirectly supports the importance of thorough concussion management: former football players had significantly more lifetime concussions, which was a significant predictor of memory problems in midlife. Proper identification and management of concussions during an athlete's playing career may help mitigate long-term cognitive risk. Current sleep difficulties and anxiety were stronger predictors of memory problems than sport history, suggesting that RTP protocols should include mental health and sleep screening, particularly following concussive events.

citation: Mack, C. D., Hershman, E. B., Anderson, R. B., Coughlin, M. J., McNitt, A. S., Sendor, R. R., & Kent, R. W. (2019). Higher rates of lower extremity injury on synthetic turf compared with natural turf among National Football League athletes: Epidemiologic confirmation of a biomechanical hypothesis. The American Journal of Sports Medicine, 47(1), 189–196.
abstract: This cohort study examined whether lower extremity (LE) injury rates in NFL regular season games (2012–2016) were higher on synthetic turf than natural turf, epidemiologically testing a prior biomechanical hypothesis that synthetic surfaces generate greater shear force on the foot due to poor cleat release. All 32 NFL teams contributed injury data under mandated consistent reporting protocols. Poisson models estimated adjusted incidence rate ratios (IRRs). Synthetic turf was associated with a 16% increase in LE injuries per play (IRR 1.16; 95% CI 1.10–1.23). This effect was more pronounced for noncontact/surface contact injuries (IRR up to 2.03 for ankle/foot with ≥8 days missed), and increased as injury locations were more distal (closer to the playing surface).
tldr: NFL players are 16% more likely to sustain a lower extremity injury per play on synthetic turf than on natural grass, with the risk rising to over 100% greater for noncontact ankle and foot injuries requiring 8+ days off. The pattern of findings—stronger associations for distal injuries and noncontact mechanisms—is consistent with the biomechanical explanation that synthetic turf fails to release cleats at injurious torque levels. Natural turf's ability to divot under load serves as a force-limiting mechanism absent in synthetic surfaces. This has direct implications for surface selection, footwear choice, and field maintenance standards.
methods: Retrospective cohort study using NFL injury surveillance data from 2012–2016 regular season (1,280 games; 213,935 plays). All 32 teams reported injuries through mandated electronic health records. LE injuries causing any missed participation or ≥8 days missed were included; subcategories of knee and ankle/foot examined. Poisson regression models estimated crude and adjusted IRRs, controlling for surface hardness (Gmax) and field moisture. Secondary analysis restricted to noncontact/surface contact injuries. Sensitivity analyses excluded potentially heterogeneous synthetic fields (Seattle, New England, Dallas).
findings: 4,801 LE injuries occurred across 1,280 games. Synthetic turf: 16% more LE injuries per play vs. natural turf (IRR 1.16; 95% CI 1.10–1.23). Effect persisted for injuries with ≥8 days missed. Noncontact/surface contact injury IRRs: overall LE 1.27 (1.15–1.41), knee 1.46 (1.20–1.77), ankle/foot 1.68 (1.36–2.08). For ≥8 days missed ankle/foot noncontact injuries: IRR 2.03 (1.42–2.89). Contact injuries attributed to player-to-player contact showed no surface difference (IRR 1.06; 0.97–1.14). Adjustment for hardness and moisture did not meaningfully alter results. If natural turf rates applied to synthetic turf games in the study, approximately 319 fewer LE injuries would have been expected.
limitations: Findings are specific to NFL conditions (elite athletes, professional maintenance, certified surfaces) and may not generalize to other levels of play or sports. Injury mechanism classification is subjective and prone to sideline reporting error. Return-to-play data may be incomplete for players who leave the league. Variability in synthetic turf design, age, and maintenance across stadiums was not fully captured. The study cannot fully account for systematic behavioral differences in play style by surface type.
practicalImplications: NFL teams and athletic staff should prioritize footwear selection optimized for synthetic surfaces, as cleat pattern significantly affects torque and shear loading. Players on synthetic turf may benefit from additional lower limb protection. Stadium operators and synthetic turf manufacturers should pursue designs that allow cleat release at injurious load thresholds, similar to natural turf divoting. Teams playing significant portions of their schedule on synthetic turf should factor surface type into injury prevention programming, including extra emphasis on ankle and foot stability training.
athleteDev: The biomechanical mechanism identified—inadequate cleat release on synthetic turf generating excess shear force—is relevant at all levels of football. Youth and collegiate programs using synthetic fields should invest in surface-appropriate footwear education and should consider this injury risk when programming high-intensity change-of-direction and sprint work. Developing players who compete on synthetic surfaces may benefit from targeted ankle/foot strength and proprioception training to compensate for the elevated mechanical demands of synthetic fields.
rtp: The elevated ankle and foot injury rates on synthetic turf—particularly for noncontact mechanisms—are directly relevant to RTP decisions. Athletes returning from lower extremity injuries (especially ankle sprains, foot injuries, or knee ligament injuries) should be cautiously managed when returning to play on synthetic surfaces. Clinicians should consider surface type as part of RTP criteria, potentially requiring higher functional thresholds before clearing athletes to compete on synthetic turf. Footwear reassessment and surface-specific functional testing should be incorporated into RTP protocols.

citation: Lonie, T. A., Brade, C. J., Finucane, M. E., Jacques, A., & Grisbrook, T. L. (2019). Hip adduction and abduction strength and adduction-to-abduction ratio changes across an Australian Football League season. Journal of Science and Medicine in Sport, 23(2), 130–135.
abstract: This cross-sectional repeated-measures study examined changes in isometric hip adduction strength, abduction strength, and adduction-to-abduction ratio across an Australian Football (AF) League season in 38 uninjured elite players from one AFL club. Maximal isometric hip strength was measured at four time points using a hand-held dynamometer with external belt fixation: start of pre-season (T1), end of pre-season (T2), mid-season (T3), and post-season (T4). Hip adduction strength and the adduction-to-abduction ratio were significantly greater at mid-season (T3) compared to pre-season (T1), while both adduction and abduction strength were significantly weaker at post-season (T4) versus T1. No differences were found between preferred and non-preferred kicking legs in adduction or abduction, though the adduction-to-abduction ratio of the preferred leg was significantly lower than the non-preferred leg at T3 and T4.
tldr: Hip adductor and abductor strength change meaningfully across an Australian Football season: adduction strength and the adduction-to-abduction ratio peak at mid-season before declining at post-season below pre-season values. Relying solely on pre-season hip strength testing to screen for groin injury risk or guide return-to-play criteria is insufficient—strength profiles shift considerably through the year. Regular in-season testing is needed to accurately reflect a player's current neuromuscular condition, and training load was not associated with these changes, suggesting that training volume alone does not explain the seasonal pattern.
methods: Cross-sectional repeated measures design. 38 elite AFL players from one club (2016 season). Maximal isometric hip adduction and abduction strength measured at T1 (start pre-season, November 2015), T2 (end pre-season, February 2016), T3 (mid-season, June 2016), and T4 (post-season, August 2016) using a hand-held dynamometer (PowerTrack II Commander, JTECH) with external belt fixation. Adduction-to-abduction ratio calculated by dividing maximal adduction by maximal abduction score. Training load quantified via session-RPE × duration. Adjusted linear mixed regression models with player characteristics (age, BM, AFL experience, games played prior to and in 2016) as covariates.
findings: Hip adduction strength was significantly greater at T3 vs. T1 (+22.71N, p<0.001) and significantly weaker at T4 vs. T1 (−18.6N, p=0.004). No significant difference between T1 and T2 for adduction. Hip abduction strength was significantly weaker at T4 vs. T1 (−24.67N, p<0.001); no difference between T1–T2 or T1–T3. Adduction-to-abduction ratio was significantly greater at T3 (+0.15N, p<0.001) vs. T1. No overall differences between preferred and non-preferred kicking legs in adduction (p=0.409) or abduction (p=0.602). However, at T3 and T4, the preferred leg adduction-to-abduction ratio was significantly lower than the non-preferred leg (T3: −0.14N, p=0.020; T4: −0.15N, p=0.019). Training load was not significantly associated with hip strength changes. Body mass was associated with abduction strength and the ratio.
limitations: Single club limits generalizability. Post-season player motivation and availability may have influenced T4 results (reduced effort anecdotally noted). Training load was not recorded during the off-season (prior to T1). The study was observational and cannot determine causation for the seasonal strength changes. No injured players were included, so findings apply only to uninjured athletes.
practicalImplications: Pre-season hip strength testing alone cannot serve as a reliable reference point for the entire season—values change significantly by mid-season and post-season. Strength and conditioning and sports medicine staff should implement regular in-season hip strength monitoring (e.g., mid-season and post-season) to track individual player changes. The preferred kicking leg shows a relatively lower adduction-to-abduction ratio at mid-season and post-season compared to the non-preferred leg, which may reflect asymmetric loading from kicking demands and could be relevant for groin injury risk stratification during the competitive season.
athleteDev: For developing AFL players, this study highlights that strength profiles are not static across a season and that pre-season screening captures only a single snapshot. Developmental programs should incorporate longitudinal strength monitoring to detect individual athletes whose adduction strength or adduction-to-abduction ratio deviates from expected seasonal trajectories. The groin-protective threshold of >80% adduction-to-abduction ratio may need to be evaluated relative to where a player is in their seasonal cycle, not just their pre-season value.
rtp: Return-to-play benchmarks for groin strain in AFL commonly use the uninjured limb as a reference, or target an adduction-to-abduction ratio of 90–100%. This study shows that hip strength symmetry and ratios fluctuate across the season in uninjured players, which has direct implications for RTP criteria. Using a pre-season value or a static normative reference may be misleading if a player's uninjured baseline has shifted. RTP assessments should ideally compare the recovering limb to in-season normative data for that athlete or to matched in-season data from uninjured teammates.
